title: Time Restricted Eating in Alzheimer's Disease (TREAD): A Pilot Study
authors_full: Gunning JA1, Hettiwatte Y1, Zaniletti I2, Smith T3, Khan N3, Aliskevich E3, Chahal G3, Demeke M3, DeCuna CJ1, Eagan D3, Krell-Roesch J4,Geda YE3, Racette SB1
authors_short: Gunning JA Hettiwatte Y Zaniletti I Smith T Khan N Aliskevich E Chahal G Demeke M DeCuna CJ Eagan D Krell-Roesch J Geda YE Racette SB

summary.hook: This poster describes a pilot study (“TREAD”) testing whether a time-restricted eating (TRE) intervention is feasible in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and exploring...
summary.summary_30s: This poster describes a pilot study (“TREAD”) testing whether a time-restricted eating (TRE) intervention is feasible in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and exploring effects on cognition and blood biomarkers relevant to Alzheimer’s disease (AD).

talktrack_2m.one_smart_follow_up: How did you operationally define a “day” for adherence (midnight-to-midnight, waking day, or individualized)?

key_numbers: TRE intervention described as “5 days/week x 12 weeks”
Adults with MCI (Mayo Clinic criteria), ages 55–89 years, BMI >18.5 and <40.0 kg/m², with a supportive study partner
8h window achieved on 78.2% of days; 10h on 94.7% of days
takeaways: Completion: N \= 27 completed participants (17 men, 10 women).
Adherence: 8h eating window achieved on 78.2% of all days; 10h eating window achieved on 94.7% of all days.
Eating-window distribution: Percent of days meeting thresholds: ≤8h 78.2%; ≤9h 90.6%; ≤10h 94.7%; ≤11h 97.8%; ≤12h 99.0%. Goal marker shown: 5/7 days/week (71%).
